```mermaid
    BR1 --> IP3[Leads to Foundational Disclosures]
```

### 4.3 R&D Funding Flow Architecture (Block-Level Functional Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FUNDING_SOURCES
        FS1[Government Grants DST DBT SERB]
        FS2[Industry CSR Funding]
        FS3[International Agencies EU Horizon NSF]
        FS4[Private Venture Capital]
    end
    subgraph RESEARCH_EXECUTION
        RE1[University Research Labs]
        RE2[Corporate R and D Centers]
        RE3[National Research Institutes]
        RE4[Public Private Partnerships]
    end
    subgraph IP_OUTPUTS
        IP1[Patent Filings]
        IP2[Research Publications]
        IP3[Trade Secrets]
        IP4[Technology Transfer Agreements]
    end
    FS1 --> RE1
    FS2 --> RE2
    FS3 --> RE3
    FS4 --> RE4
    RE1 --> IP1
    RE1 --> IP2
    RE2 --> IP1
    RE2 --> IP3
    RE3 --> IP1
    RE3 --> IP4
    RE4 --> IP1
    RE4 --> IP4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers above use purely alphanumeric prefixes (`FS1`, `RE2`, `IP3`, etc.) to comply with the V10 engine anti-collision safeguard. Reserved keywords like `end` and `subgraph` are not used as standalone node labels.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define technological research and differentiate between basic and applied research.**

**Model Answer:**
Technological research is the systematic scientific inquiry aimed at generating new engineering knowledge, products, or processes. Basic research is curiosity-driven and theory-oriented (e.g., studying superconductivity at room temperature) with no immediate commercial objective. Applied research is problem-specific and target-oriented (e.g., developing a low-cost water purifier), aiming for practical deployment within a defined timeframe.

| Parameter | Basic Research | Applied Research |
|---|---|---|
| Goal | Knowledge expansion | Solution delivery |
| Timeline | Long (5–20 years) | Short-Medium (1–5 years) |
| Risk | Very High | Moderate |
| Patent Potential | Indirect | Direct |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for differentiation table)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the significance of prior art search in technological research.**

**Model Answer:**
Prior art search is the systematic review of existing public disclosures — patents, journal articles, conference proceedings, and product manuals — to establish the novelty of a proposed invention. It serves three functions: (i) prevents redundant R&D expenditure, (ii) strengthens the patent application's novelty claim, and (iii) reduces litigation risk post-grant. Standard repositories include WIPO PATENTSCOPE, USPTO, EPO Espacenet, and Indian Patent Advanced Search System (IPAS).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for three-fold significance)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various stages of the technological research lifecycle. Discuss how each stage contributes to eventual patent filing. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Stage 1 — Problem Identification (1 mark):**
Research begins with the identification of a technical gap or unmet industrial need. Tools such as literature survey, market analysis, and stakeholder interviews are deployed. The output is a well-defined research question with measurable objectives.

**Stage 2 — Hypothesis Formulation (1 mark):**
The research question is converted into a testable hypothesis using the SMART framework. A weak hypothesis produces weak downstream patents.

**Stage 3 — Methodology Design (1 mark):**
Experimental protocols, simulation parameters, and data acquisition methods are documented. Reproducibility — a legal requirement under patent law — is established here.

**Stage 4 — Data Collection and Analysis (1.5 marks):**
Empirical results are gathered using calibrated instruments. Statistical tools such as ANOVA, t-tests, and regression are applied. Negative results are equally valuable and must be documented.

**Stage 5 — Disclosure and Patent Filing (2.5 marks):**
The validated invention is disclosed internally, the lab notebook is signed and witnessed, and a provisional patent specification is filed with the Indian Patent Office within 12 months to establish priority. Each upstream stage feeds a specific patentability criterion:
- Stage 1 → Industrial Applicability
- Stage 2 → Inventive Step
- Stage 3 → Sufficiency of Disclosure
- Stage 4 → Reproducibility
- Stage 5 → Priority Date

**(b)** Discuss the role of research documentation in protecting intellectual property rights. What are the best practices for maintaining laboratory notebooks? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Research Documentation in IPR (3.5 marks):**
1. **Establishes Priority Date:** The first signed and witnessed notebook entry can serve as prior art evidence in patent disputes.
2. **Supports Inventorship Claims:** Helps resolve disputes over who conceived the invention.
3. **Enables Patent Prosecution:** Detailed records strengthen the patent specification.
4. **Defends Against Challenges:** Provides documentary proof of the invention's date of conception and reduction to practice.

**Best Practices for Laboratory Notebooks (3.5 marks):**
1. Use **permanently bound notebooks** with pre-numbered pages.
2. Record entries in **permanent ink** with date and time stamps.
3. Have each entry **witnessed and signed** by a non-inventor colleague.
4. Leave **no blank spaces**; draw lines through unused portions.
5. Attach and sign **supporting data printouts, photographs, and graphs**.
6. Maintain **separate notebooks for each research project** to avoid intermingling.
7. Store notebooks in a **secure, access-controlled repository** with digital backup.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** What is R&D intensity? Compute the R&D intensity of a company that spent ₹75 crore on research and reported total revenue of ₹1500 crore. Comment on whether this intensity is competitive globally. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
R&D Intensity is the ratio of R&D expenditure to total revenue, expressed as a percentage. It measures a company's commitment to innovation.

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{RD} &= \frac{E_{RD}}{R_{total}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Substitution and Computation (3 marks):**

Given:
- $E_{RD} = 75$ crore INR
- $R_{total} = 1500$ crore INR

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{RD} &= \frac{75}{1500} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{1}{20} \times 100\% \\
&= 5.0\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Global Benchmarking (2.5 marks):**
Global R&D-intensive industries typically report intensities as follows:
- Pharmaceuticals: 15–20%
- Semiconductor: 15–25%
- Software / IT: 12–18%
- Automotive: 4–6%
- General Manufacturing: 2–4%

A value of **5.0%** places the company in the **competitive automotive / advanced manufacturing band**, comparable to global leaders such as Toyota (≈4.0%) and Bosch (≈6.5%). However, it would be considered **suboptimal** for a pharmaceutical or semiconductor firm.

**(b)** Explain the concept of Technology Readiness Level (TRL). Map each TRL stage to its corresponding research activity. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (2 marks):**
TRL is a 9-point scale originally developed by NASA and now adopted by the European Union Horizon programs and DST India to assess the maturity of a technology from concept to commercial deployment.

**Mapping Table (5 marks):**

| TRL | Stage Name | Research Activity | Patent Output Potential |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic Principles Observed | Fundamental scientific discovery | Low |
| 2 | Technology Concept Formulated | Hypothesis and proof-of-concept sketch | Low |
| 3 | Experimental Proof of Concept | Laboratory validation | Medium |
| 4 | Lab-Scale Validation | Component testing in controlled environment | Medium |
| 5 | Relevant Environment Validation | Prototype testing in simulated conditions | High |
| 6 | Pilot-Scale Demonstration | Engineering-scale prototype | High |
| 7 | Operational Prototype | Field testing under real conditions | Very High |
| 8 | System Complete and Qualified | Certification and standards compliance | Very High |
| 9 | Commercial Deployment | Market launch and scale-up | Maintenance Patents |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Students frequently confuse **Patent Filing** with **Patent Grant**. Always distinguish between the two.
> 2. Avoid writing "research leads to innovation" generically — examiners reward **specific TRL stage mappings**.
> 3. Numerical answers (such as R&D Intensity) must show the **complete algebraic substitution**, not just the final number.
> 4. Never use the pipe symbol `|` inside markdown tables for absolute value. Always use `\vert` or `\mid`.
> 5. Do not confuse **Trade Secret** with **Patent** — the former has no time-bound protection but the latter does.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Technological Research** is the upstream genesis of all patentable innovation.
- The **five-stage lifecycle** is: Problem → Hypothesis → Method → Data → Disclosure.
- **Prior Art Search** is non-negotiable before patent filing — use WIPO PATENTSCOPE and IPAS.
- **R&D Intensity** formula: $I_{RD} = \dfrac{E_{RD}}{R_{total}} \times 100\%$.
- **Novelty Index** threshold for strong patentability: $NI \geq 0.7$.
- **TRL Scale** ranges from 1 (basic principles) to 9 (commercial deployment).
- **Research documentation** must be signed, dated, witnessed, and reproducible.
- **Basic Research** → curiosity; **Applied Research** → problem-solving; **Developmental Research** → commercialization.
- A **provisional patent specification** must be filed within **12 months** of public disclosure to preserve priority.
- KTU examiners reward **numerical examples, tables, and mermaid diagrams** in long-answer responses.
- Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX expressions inside markdown tables.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inventions and Innovations - Important Examples of IPR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT744 - PATENTS AND IPR
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inventions and Innovations - Important Examples of IPR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Invention — Formal Academic Definition

An **Invention** is a novel, non-obvious, and useful creation of the human intellect that provides a new technical solution to a specific problem in the field of technology. Under the Indian Patents Act, **1970 (as amended in 2005)**, an invention is defined under **Section 2(1)(j)** as:

> *"A new product or process involving an inventive step and capable of industrial application."*

Three legal pillars must coexist for any creation to qualify as a patentable invention:
1. **Novelty** — never made public before the priority date.
2. **Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness)** — not obvious to a person skilled in the art (PHOSITA).
3. **Industrial Applicability** — capable of being made or used in an industry.

### 1.2 Innovation — Formal Academic Definition

**Innovation**, as defined by **Joseph Schumpeter** (1934) in *The Theory of Economic Development*, is the process by which an invention is successfully introduced into the market, society, or industry to create **economic value** or **social impact**. KTU 2024 emphasises that innovation = Invention **+** Commercialisation **+** Adoption.

| Parameter | Invention | Innovation |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Idea/Lab | Marketplace |
| End Goal | Technical novelty | Value creation |
| Risk | Technical | Commercial + Technical |
| Output | Patent/prototype | Product/Service |
| Stage | Concept | Diffusion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Line:** "All inventions are NOT innovations, but every innovation originates from an invention (or a combination of inventions)."

### 1.3 Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) — Definition

**Intellectual Property Rights (IPR)** are the legally recognised exclusive rights vested in the creator over their intangible creations of the mind, granting the right-holder the authority to control the use, commercialisation, and dissemination of their intellectual output for a limited statutory period.

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The House of Creations":**
> Think of the human mind as a **factory**. Inside this factory:
> - **Raw Material (Idea)** → enters the factory floor.
> - **Invention** = the **blueprint** drawn by the engineer — a technical design (e.g., a new circuit).
> - **Innovation** = the **finished house built** from that blueprint and sold in the market.
> - **IPR** = the **title deed and fence** around the house — legally protecting it from being copied or trespassed.
>
> Without the fence (IPR), anyone could replicate the house for free; without the blueprint (Invention), there is nothing to build; without the building (Innovation), the blueprint has no economic value.

### 1.5 Salient Metrics in IPR (Bold Constants)

- **TRIPS Agreement** (1994) under WTO sets the **minimum 20-year patent term** globally.
- **Berne Convention** (1886) — copyright auto-protection for the **life of the author + 60 years** (in India).
- **Paris Convention** (1883) — first major international treaty for **industrial property**, establishing the **right of priority (12 months)**.
- **Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT)** — international patent filing route administered by WIPO, governed by **35 Articles**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Intellectual Property Rights

KTU 2024 Module 1 classifies IPR into **eight principal branches**. Each branch protects a distinct class of intellectual creation:

1. **Patents** — protect inventions (products & processes).
2. **Copyrights** — protect literary, artistic, musical, and software works.
3. **Trademarks** — protect distinctive signs, logos, brand identifiers.
4. **Industrial Designs** — protect the aesthetic appearance of an article.
5. **Geographical Indications (GI)** — protect goods tied to a specific region.
6. **Trade Secrets** — protect confidential business information.
7. **Plant Breeders' Rights** — protect new plant varieties.
8. **Semiconductor Integrated Circuit Layout-Design Rights** — protect IC topographies.

### 2.2 Five Classic Examples That Define Each IPR Category

| # | Landmark Example | Year | Type of IPR | Country/Region | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wright Brothers' Flying Machine** | 1903–1906 | Patent (US 821,393) | USA | First powered aircraft patent — birth of aviation IP. |
| 2 | **Apple iPhone Multi-Touch (US 7,479,949)** | 2008 | Patent | USA | Revolutionised smartphone UX; led to Samsung litigation. |
| 3 | **Coca-Cola Formula (Trade Secret)** | 1886 → present | Trade Secret | USA (Global) | Famous formula never patented; protected as a trade secret. |
| 4 | **Basmati Rice (GI Tag)** | 2016 (India) | Geographical Indication | India | India's first GI for Basmati; contested by US (RiceTec). |
| 5 | **Harry Potter (Copyright)** | 1997 | Copyright | UK (Berne) | Auto-protected globally; translated into 80+ languages. |
| 6 | **Microsoft Windows Source Code** | 1985 → present | Trade Secret + Copyright | USA | Dual protection — code is copyrighted, algorithms kept secret. |
| 7 | **Taj Mahal Architectural Design** | 2001 | Copyright (artistic work) | India | Architectural works protected under Indian Copyright Act 1957. |
| 8 | **Intel x86 Microprocessor** | 1978 | Patent + Trade Secret | USA | Defines the modern computing era; billions in licensing revenue. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** When KTU asks "Give an example of IPR", always answer with **name, year, type of IPR, and the specific clause/section** under Indian law. Generic answers fetch only partial marks.

### 2.3 KTU Cheat Sheet — Key Statutory Periods

| IPR Type | Statutory Term in India | Governing Statute | Key Section |
|---|---|---|---|
| Patent | **20 years** from filing date | Patents Act, 1970 | Sec 2(1)(j), Sec 53 |
| Copyright | **Life of author + 60 years** | Copyright Act, 1957 | Sec 13, Sec 22 |
| Trademark | **10 years** (renewable) | Trade Marks Act, 1999 | Sec 23, Sec 25 |
| Industrial Design | **10 years** (extendable to 15) | Designs Act, 2000 | Sec 11 |
| Geographical Indication | **10 years** (renewable) | GI of Goods Act, 1999 | Sec 9 |
| Plant Variety | **15–18 years** (crop dependent) | PPV&FR Act, 2001 | Sec 24 |
| Semiconductor IC | **10 years** | Semiconductor IC Act, 2000 | Sec 7 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Design Patent (USA)** with **Industrial Design Registration (India)**. India does not have design patents — designs are registered under the Designs Act, 2000. This is a frequent MCQ trap.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of These IPR Examples

- **Information Technology (CS/AI/ML):** Software is dual-protected — the **source code** under copyright and the **algorithm/method** under patent (subject matter eligibility is jurisdiction-dependent).
- **Biotechnology:** Recombinant DNA technology (Cohen-Boyer patent, 1980) pioneered the biotech patent era.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** CAD/CAM innovations rely on **design registrations** + **trade secret protection** for manufacturing know-how.
- **Civil Engineering:** Construction methodologies (e.g., pre-cast concrete) attract process patents; building facades may qualify for design rights.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough of Landmark Examples

### 3.1 Analytical Case 1: The Wright Brothers' Patent (Birth of Aviation IP)

**Background Timeline:**
- 1903 (Dec 17) — First successful powered flight at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina.
- 1904 — Initial patent application filed.
- 1906 (May 22) — **US Patent 821,393** granted for *"Flying-Machine"*.

**Step-by-Step Analysis of Why It Qualifies as a Patentable Invention:**

1. **Problem Identification:** Existing gliders lacked sustained powered control — they could not be steered effectively in three axes (pitch, roll, yaw).
2. **Technical Solution:** Invented **wing-warping** for roll control, combined with a movable rudder for yaw coordination.
3. **Novelty Check:** No prior public disclosure matched this three-axis control system. Search of US patent archives confirmed uniqueness.
4. **Non-Obviousness Test:** A PHOSITA in 1903 aeronautics would NOT have readily combined wing-warping with rudder linkage — the linkage ratio was non-trivial.
5. **Industrial Applicability:** The invention was demonstrably reduced to practice and capable of replication for commercial aviation.

$$\text{Patentability Score} = f(\text{Novelty}, \text{Inventive Step}, \text{Industrial Application})$$

$$\text{Result: } \forall \text{ (Wright Brothers)} : \text{Novelty} = \text{True} \land \text{Inventive Step} = \text{True} \land \text{Industrial Application} = \text{True}$$

Hence, the **f(P) = 1 (Patentable)**.

### 3.2 Analytical Case 2: Coca-Cola — Why a Trade Secret, Not a Patent?

**Reasoning Chain:**

1. **Coca-Cola's formula** was invented by **John Pemberton** in **1886**.
2. If patented, the protection would have expired after **20 years** (i.e., by 1906).
3. Instead, Coca-Cola chose to keep the formula as a **Trade Secret** — never disclosed, accessed by only a few employees under strict NDAs.
4. **Trade Secret protection lasts indefinitely**, as long as reasonable steps are taken to maintain secrecy.

$$\text{Value}_{\text{Trade Secret}} = \frac{\text{Revenue per year} \times \text{Time horizon}}{\text{Disclosure risk} \times \text{Replication cost}}$$

Because Coca-Cola's brand recognition and marketing moat are stronger than the recipe itself, the **trade secret route dominates** the patent route. This is a classic application of **Game Theory** in IP strategy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Trade secrets protect *information*; patents protect *technical solutions*. Trade secrets have **no time limit** but offer **no protection against independent reverse engineering** — a key exam contrast point.

### 3.3 Analytical Case 3: Apple vs Samsung — Modern Patent War

**Step-by-Step Analysis of the Litigation Logic:**

1. Apple filed a **utility patent (US 7,479,949)** in **2004** for **"Touch screen device, method, and graphical user interface for determining commands by applying heuristics"** — the famous pinch-to-zoom patent.
2. Samsung released the **Galaxy S** in **2010**, allegedly using similar multi-touch gestures.
3. Apple sued Samsung in **April 2011** in the Northern District of California.
4. Jury awarded Apple **$1.05 billion** in damages (August 2012).
5. Damages were later reduced and partially remanded; final settlement reached in **2018** with undisclosed terms.

$$\text{Litigation Outcome} = \text{Infringement Found} \times \text{Damages}_{\text{base}} \times (1 + \text{Willful Multiplier})$$

$$\text{Samsung Original Verdict} = \$1{,}049{,}343{,}540$$

This case demonstrates the **commercial weaponisation of patents** — modern innovation strategy involves building **patent thickets** (large overlapping patent portfolios) for defensive and offensive purposes.

### 3.4 Analytical Case 4: Basmati GI Tag — India vs RiceTec

1. **Basmati** is a long-grain aromatic rice grown primarily in the **Indo-Gangetic plains** (Punjab, Haryana, UP, J&K in India, and parts of Pakistan).
2. In **1997**, the US company **RiceTec** attempted to patent "Basmati" rice lines and grains.
3. India challenged the patent under the **geographical indication** principle — Basmati is intrinsically tied to its geographic origin.
4. **2016**: India granted its **first GI tag for Basmati** (Certificate No. 145).
5. **Outcome:** RiceTec lost broad "Basmati" claims, retaining only limited hybrid line patents.

$$\text{GI Eligibility} = \text{Origin}_{\text{quality}} \times \text{Reputation}_{\text{geographic}} \times \text{Traditional Knowledge}_{\text{link}}$$

This is a textbook case for **the intersection of traditional knowledge and modern IP law** — a key KTU Module 1 application area.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**

**Q1. Define an "invention" as per the Indian Patents Act, 1970. List the three essential criteria for patentability.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
As per **Section 2(1)(j)** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970, an invention means a new product or process involving an inventive step and capable of industrial application. The three essential criteria for patentability are:
1. **Novelty** (Section 2(1)(l)) — the invention must not have been published or used anywhere in the world before the priority date.
2. **Inventive Step / Non-Obviousness** (Section 2(1(ja)) — the invention must not be obvious to a person skilled in the art.
3. **Industrial Applicability** (Section 2(1)(ac)) — the invention must be capable of being made or used in an industry.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between an "invention" and an "innovation" with one real-world example for each.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- **Invention** is the creation of a new technical idea, product, or process. *Example:* Alexander Graham Bell's telephone (US Patent 174,465, 1876).
- **Innovation** is the successful commercialisation and societal adoption of that invention. *Example:* The **iPhone by Apple (2007)** is an innovation built on the prior invention of multi-touch technology (US 7,479,949).
- The key distinction is that invention is **idea-centric** (lab-stage) whereas innovation is **market-centric** (commercialised diffusion).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Question (14 Marks)

> **[KTU ESE — Module 1, Internal Choice Provided]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Intellectual Property Rights (IPR). Discuss in detail the major categories of IPR recognised under the Indian legal framework, with at least one illustrative example for each category. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With suitable examples, differentiate between the following pairs:
- (i) Patent vs Trade Secret
- (ii) Copyright vs Trademark
- (iii) Industrial Design vs Geographical Indication *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (a)** *(7 Marks)*

**Definition (1 mark):**
Intellectual Property Rights are the legally protected rights granted to creators/owners of intangible intellectual creations, enabling them to control the use of their creations for a defined statutory period.

**Major Categories (6 marks):**

| Category | Example | Term |
|---|---|---|
| **Patent** | Wright Brothers' Flying Machine (US 821,393) | 20 years |
| **Copyright** | Harry Potter novels by J.K. Rowling | Life + 60 years |
| **Trademark** | Coca-Cola wordmark & Spencerian script logo | 10 years (renewable) |
| **Industrial Design** | Eames Lounge Chair design | 10 years (+5 extendable) |
| **Geographical Indication** | Basmati Rice (India GI Registry 2016) | 10 years (renewable) |
| **Trade Secret** | Coca-Cola formula (since 1886) | Indefinite (subject to secrecy) |
| **Plant Breeders' Rights** | Bt Cotton in India | 15–18 years |
| **Semiconductor IC Layout** | Intel x86 microprocessor design | 10 years |

The Indian legislative framework includes: **Patents Act 1970, Copyright Act 1957, Trade Marks Act 1999, Designs Act 2000, GI of Goods Act 1999, PPV&FR Act 2001, Semiconductor IC Act 2000**.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (b)** *(7 Marks)*

**(i) Patent vs Trade Secret (2.5 marks):**

$$\text{Patent: Technical Disclosure} \xrightarrow{\text{20 years}} \text{Monopoly Right}$$

$$\text{Trade Secret: Indefinite Secrecy} \xrightarrow{\text{no disclosure}} \text{No Monopoly but Indefinite}$$

- **Patent** requires public disclosure; **Trade Secret** forbids it.
- **Example:** Apple's iPhone (patent) vs Coca-Cola's formula (trade secret).

**(ii) Copyright vs Trademark (2.5 marks):**

- **Copyright** protects original literary, artistic, musical works — auto-arises on creation. Example: Harry Potter novels.
- **Trademark** protects distinctive brand identifiers — requires registration for strong enforcement. Example: Nike Swoosh.
- Copyright is **creative-content-centric**; Trademark is **source-identifier-centric**.

**(iii) Industrial Design vs Geographical Indication (2 marks):**

- **Industrial Design** protects the **aesthetic appearance** of a manufactured article (e.g., a car body shape). Granted to an individual/company.
- **Geographical Indication** protects the **reputation/quality** of goods tied to a **specific geographic region** (e.g., Darjeeling Tea, Champagne). Granted to a producer association from that region.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the landmark case of *Apple Inc. v. Samsung Electronics Co.* highlighting how patents on the iPhone's multi-touch technology reshaped the global smartphone industry. Evaluate the strategic lessons for engineering businesses. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With the help of the Wright Brothers' patent and the Basmati Rice GI tag, explain how the **type of IPR chosen** is influenced by the **nature of the creation** and its **commercialisation strategy**. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (a)** *(7 Marks)*

**Background (2 marks):** Apple obtained US Patent 7,479,949 (filed 2004, granted 2008) for **multi-touch gesture technology** (pinch-to-zoom, swipe-to-unlock). Following the 2010 launch of Samsung's Galaxy S, Apple filed suit in 2011 alleging infringement.

**Court Outcome (2 marks):** Initial jury verdict awarded Apple **$1.05 billion in damages** (Aug 2012), later partially reduced. Final settlement reached in 2018 with undisclosed terms. The case set precedent for **software-related patents** in the US.

**Strategic Lessons (3 marks):**
1. **Patent Thickets:** Modern tech firms build large overlapping patent portfolios as both sword and shield.
2. **First-Mover Advantage:** The first to patent a foundational technology gains **blocking power** over competitors.
3. **Cross-Licensing:** In saturated markets, firms often prefer **cross-licensing** to litigation, reducing the value of pure patent dominance.
4. **Geographic Strategy:** Patents must be filed **country-wise**; Indian, US, and EU grants are independent.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (b)** *(7 Marks)*

**Wright Brothers' Patent (3.5 marks):**
The Wright Brothers' flying machine was a **technical solution to the problem of controlled powered flight**. Because the invention's value lay in its **technical configuration** (three-axis control), and because it could be reverse-engineered by competitors if publicly disclosed, the creators chose the **patent route** to obtain a 20-year statutory monopoly in exchange for full public disclosure of the mechanism. This aligns with the *patenting strategy* where the technical contribution is the core value.

**Basmati Rice GI Tag (3.5 marks):**
Basmati Rice, by contrast, is a **natural agricultural product** whose value arises from the **unique climatic and soil conditions of the Indo-Gangetic plains** plus centuries of farmer knowledge. There is no single "inventor". Hence, a **patent would be inappropriate** (no single human inventor), and a **trade secret would be infeasible** (the rice grains are publicly traded). The **Geographical Indication** route is the only legal mechanism that protects **collective regional reputation** from misappropriation (e.g., by RiceTec USA).

**Strategic Synthesis:**
- **Patent** → when the creation is a **novel technical solution** by a defined inventor.
- **Trade Secret** → when the creation is **process know-how** that can be kept confidential.
- **GI** → when the creation is a **reputation tied to geography and tradition**.
- **Copyright** → when the creation is an **original expressive work**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PEBBT744 Answers:**
> 1. **Conflating "Patent" with "Design Patent"** — India has NO design patents; designs fall under the Designs Act 2000. ❌
> 2. **Forgetting to cite the relevant section** (e.g., Section 2(1)(j) for invention definition). Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing statutory references.
> 3. **Generic example dumping** — listing "Apple" or "Coca-Cola" without specifying the **patent number, year, and type of IPR** is a 50% mark-loss.
> 4. **Confusing TRIPS (WTO, 1994) with PCT (WIPO, 1970)** — TRIPS sets minimum IP standards; PCT is a *filing* mechanism.
> 5. **Writing "life + 60 years"** for software copyright in India but failing to mention it applies to *"literary works" under Sec 2(o)*.
> 6. **Missing the "Innovation ≠ Invention" distinction** — the Schumpeterian definition is worth 2 marks alone in 14-mark questions.
> 7. **Omitting the international treaty names** — KTU explicitly tests the *Berne Convention, Paris Convention, PCT, TRIPS* linkage.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1**

- **Invention** = Section 2(1)(j), Patents Act 1970 → requires **Novelty + Inventive Step + Industrial Application**.
- **Innovation** = Schumpeter (1934) = Invention **+** Commercialisation **+** Adoption.
- **IPR** = 8 main branches: **Patent, Copyright, Trademark, Industrial Design, GI, Trade Secret, Plant Breeders' Rights, Semiconductor IC Layout**.
- **Patent term** = **20 years** (TRIPS-mandated global minimum).
- **Copyright term** = **Life of author + 60 years** in India (Berne Convention compliance).
- **Trademark** = **10 years** renewable indefinitely; ™ vs ® distinction.
- **GI** = **10 years** renewable; protects **regional reputation** (e.g., Basmati, Champagne, Darjeeling Tea).
- **Trade Secret** = **Indefinite** term, no registration; loses protection on independent reverse engineering.
- **Key Conventions:** **Berne (1886)**, **Paris (1883)**, **PCT (1970)**, **TRIPS (1994)**, **UPOV (1961)**.
- **Landmark Examples to Memorise:** Wright Brothers (1906), Apple v Samsung (2012), Coca-Cola (1886), Basmati GI (2016), Cohen-Boyer r-DNA Patent (1980), Diamond v Chakrabarty (1980, US Supreme Court — first living-organism patent).
- **Strategic Insight:** The choice of IPR is governed by **creation type, market strategy, secrecy feasibility, and geographic scope**.
- **Mathematical IP Valuation Formula:**
$$\text{IP Value} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\text{Cash Flow}_t \times \text{Royalty Rate}}{(1+r)^t}$$
where $T$ is the protection term, $r$ is the discount rate.
- **KTU Mantra:** "**Cite the Section. Cite the Year. Cite the Example.**" — the three-legged stool of high marks in PEBBT744.

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Registration of IPRs"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Registration of Intellectual Property Rights (IPRs) is the statutory, administrative, and procedural mechanism through which an applicant, inventor, creator, or authorized assignee seeks formal recognition, exclusive ownership, and legally enforceable monopoly rights over an intangible creation of the human intellect, before the designated national or international IP registry, in compliance with the governing IP statute of the respective jurisdiction.*

In plain English: **Registration is the "birth certificate" of an intellectual asset.** Just as you register a car to claim ownership, you register an idea, brand, design, or invention with the government to prove it is *yours* and that *nobody else can legally copy, use, or sell it* without your permission.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you invented a unique flavor of **Kerala banana chips** that tastes like *gulkand and honey*. Three possibilities exist:

1. **No registration** → Anyone can copy your flavor next month. You have no legal proof of being first.
2. **Informal secrecy (Trade Secret)** → You keep the recipe locked in a vault. Protection lasts only as long as nobody reverse-engineers it.
3. **Formal registration** → You file a **Patent** (for the process), a **Trademark** (for the brand name), and a **Geographical Indication** (for "Kerala Banana Chips"). Now you have **three layers of government-backed legal protection** that you can *enforce in court*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** Not all IPRs *require* registration to exist. **Copyright arises automatically** upon creation. But **registration makes enforcement dramatically easier** and is mandatory for **Patents, Trademarks, Industrial Designs, and Geographical Indications**.

### 1.3 The Six IPRs Covered in This Module

| # | IPR Type | Subject Matter | Statutory Basis (India) | Registration Mandatory? |
|---|----------|---------------|-------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Copyright** | Literary, artistic, musical, cinematographic, software | Copyright Act, **1957** | Optional (but highly recommended) |
| 2 | **Trademark** | Brand name, logo, slogan, sound mark, color | Trade Marks Act, **1999** | **Yes** (for ® symbol) |
| 3 | **Patent** | Invention (product or process) | Patents Act, **1970** | **Yes** (mandatory) |
| 4 | **Geographical Indication (GI)** | Goods originating from a specific region | Geographical Indications of Goods Act, **1999** | **Yes** (mandatory) |
| 5 | **Trade Secret** | Confidential business information | Common law / Contract law | **No formal registration** |
| 6 | **Industrial Design** | Aesthetic appearance of an article | Designs Act, **2000** | **Yes** (mandatory) |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric for KTU:** Under the **Madrid Protocol** (international trademark) the standard protection period is **10 years**, renewable. Under the **PCT (Patent Cooperation Treaty)**, the international phase gives **30/31 months** from priority date to enter national phases. Under the **Hague Agreement** (industrial designs), initial protection is **5 years** renewable up to **15 years**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Unified Theory of IP Registration

Despite the apparent diversity, every IP registration system follows **5 universal operational phases**:

1. **Determination of Registrability** → Is the subject matter legally eligible? (novelty, non-obviousness, distinctiveness, etc.)
2. **Application Filing** → Submission of prescribed forms, fees, and documents to the registry.
3. **Formal Examination** → The registry checks procedural compliance, classification, and substantive criteria.
4. **Publication & Opposition** → The application is published in the official journal; third parties may oppose within a statutory window.
5. **Grant / Registration Certificate** → Upon overcoming objections, the registry issues a registration certificate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Phase (KTU Board-Examiner Expectation):**
> - *Registrability* prevents the registry from wasting resources on ineligible matter.
> - *Filing* creates a **priority date** (the date that decides who wins in a race between two applicants).
> - *Examination* filters out frivolous or fraudulent claims.
> - *Publication & Opposition* satisfies the principle of **natural justice** — the public must be informed before a monopoly is granted.
> - *Registration* creates a **public record** and an **incontestable right** after the prescribed period.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

### 2.2.1 Copyright Registration

| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| **Governing Law** | Copyright Act, 1957 (as amended in 2012) |
| **Authority** | Copyright Office, New Delhi (under Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade) |
| **Form Used** | **Form XIV** (for registration of copyright) |
| **Fees Range** | ₹500 (for literary/artistic/dramatic/musical works per work) to ₹2,000–₹4,000 (cinematograph/sound recordings) |
| **Registration Time** | Approximately **12–18 months** (no fixed statutory timeline) |
| **Term of Protection** | **Life of the author + 60 years** (literary/artistic/dramatic/musical); **60 years from publication** (cinematograph/sound recording/photograph); **60 years from the beginning of the calendar year** (anonymous/pseudonymous works) |
| **Automatic Protection?** | **Yes** — copyright subsists the moment work is created and fixed in a tangible medium |
| **International Treaty** | **Berne Convention** (1886); **Universal Copyright Convention (UCC)**; **TRIPS Agreement**; **WIPO Copyright Treaty (WCT)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** Registering a copyright in India is a **prerequisite for filing a suit for infringement** in some interpretations, though the Copyright Act of 2012 made registration *facilitative* rather than *mandatory* for subsistence of rights. However, registration serves as **prima facie evidence** in court.

### 2.2.2 Trademark Registration

| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| **Governing Law** | Trade Marks Act, 1999 (Rules 2002, amended 2017) |
| **Authority** | **Registrar of Trademarks**, **Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM)**, Mumbai; regional offices at Delhi, Kolkata, Chennai, Ahmedabad |
| **Form Used** | **Form TM-A** (application) |
| **Trademark Classes** | **45 classes** (1–34 = goods; 35–45 = services) under **Nice Classification (11th Edition)** |
| **Fees** | ₹4,500 (e-filing for individual/startup) to ₹10,000 (physical filing) |
| **Examination Timeline** | Statutory **3 months** for examination; total registration ~ **8–12 months** if unopposed |
| **Term of Protection** | **10 years** from date of application, **renewable indefinitely** for successive 10-year terms |
| **Symbols** | ™ (unregistered); ® (registered only) |
| **International Treaties** | **Madrid Protocol** (1989); **Nice Agreement**; **Vienna Agreement**; **Paris Convention**; **TRIPS** |
| **Well-Known Marks** | Section 2(1)(zg) of TM Act, 1999 — protected even if not registered in India (e.g., *Coca-Cola*, *Apple*) |

### 2.2.3 Patent Registration

| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| **Governing Law** | Patents Act, 1970 (amended 2005 to comply with TRIPS) |
| **Authority** | **Patent Office, Kolkata** (head); branches at Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai |
| **Form Used** | **Form 1** (application); **Form 2** (provisional/complete specification); **Form 5** (declaration of inventorship) |
| **Fees** | ₹1,600 (e-filing, natural person) to ₹8,000 (e-filing, legal entity); **80% fee reduction for startups, small entities, and female applicants** |
| **Substantive Criteria** | **Novelty**, **Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness)**, **Industrial Application (Utility)** — Section 2(1)(j), 2(1)(ja), 2(1)(ac) |
| **Examination Timeline** | Form 18 filing → **First Examination Report in 12–18 months**; total grant ~ **3–5 years** |
| **Term of Protection** | **20 years** from date of filing (subject to annual renewal fees from year 3) |
| **International Treaties** | **PCT (Patent Cooperation Treaty)**; **Paris Convention**; **Budapest Treaty** (microorganisms); **TRIPS** |
| **PCT Route Timeline** | Priority date → **30/31 months** to enter national phase |

> [!WARNING]
> **What is NOT Patentable in India (Section 3 & 4):** Frivolous inventions, inventions contrary to public order/morality, mere discovery of scientific principles, atomic energy-related inventions, plants/animals (other than microorganisms), mathematical/business methods, mental/religious methods, traditional knowledge, and **pre-grant opposition** under Section 25(1) is available.

### 2.2.4 Geographical Indication (GI) Registration

| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| **Governing Law** | Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999 |
| **Authority** | **Geographical Indications Registry, Chennai** |
| **Form Used** | **Form GI-1** (application); **Form GI-2** (statement of case) |
| **Who Can Apply?** | **Association of persons / producers / organization / authority** — *individuals cannot apply* |
| **Fees** | ₹5,000 (natural person, single class); higher for legal entities |
| **Term of Protection** | **10 years**, renewable indefinitely |
| **Examples** | *Darjeeling Tea*, *Kanchipuram Silk*, *Alphonso Mango*, *Banaras Brocades*, *Aranmula Kannadi*, *Kerala Mural Painting*, *Palakkadan Matta Rice* |
| **International Treaties** | **Lisbon Agreement** (1958); **Madrid Agreement**; **TRIPS** (Articles 22–24); Geneva Act of the Lisbon Agreement (2015) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** A GI is a **collective right** belonging to a *community or group of producers*, not an individual. It prevents misuse of the regional name by outsiders.

### 2.2.5 Trade Secret (Registration NOT Required)

| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| **Governing Law** | **No dedicated statute** in India. Protected under **common law breach of confidence**, **contract law**, **Information Technology Act, 2000** (Sections 66, 72, 72A), and **Copyright Act** (for compilations) |
| **How Created?** | Through **confidentiality agreements (NDAs)**, **non-compete clauses**, and **physical/digital security measures** |
| **Term of Protection** | **Indefinite**, as long as the information remains secret and reasonable steps are taken to protect it |
| **Famous Examples** | **Coca-Cola formula** (>130 years); **Google's search algorithm**; **KFC's 11 herbs and spices** |
| **Remedy on Breach** | Civil suit for injunction + damages; criminal under IT Act in some cases |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade Secrets vs. Patents — KTU Board Favorite Question:**
> - **Patent** = public disclosure, 20-year monopoly, requires novelty.
> - **Trade Secret** = NO public disclosure, indefinite protection, no registration, lost once independently reverse-engineered.
> - **Rule of thumb:** If you can keep it secret → trade secret. If you must disclose it → patent it.

### 2.2.6 Industrial Design Registration

| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| **Governing Law** | Designs Act, 2000 |
| **Authority** **Patent Office, Kolkata (Designs Wing)** |
| **Form Used** | **Form 1** (application); **Form 18** (claim of priority under Paris Convention) |
| **Locarno Classification** | **32 classes**; **80 subclasses** |
| **Fees** | ₹1,000 (e-filing for natural person); ₹4,000 (legal entity) |
| **Term of Protection** | **10 years**, extendable by **5 more years** (total **15 years** maximum) |
| **Criteria** | **Novelty**, **Originality**, applied to a **functional article** (designs of pure utility or those dictated solely by function are excluded) |
| **International Treaties** | **Hague Agreement (Geneva Act, 1999)**; **Paris Convention**; **Locarno Agreement** |
| **Examples** | Apple's iPhone shape, Coca-Cola bottle, car body styling, furniture design |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Walkthroughs

This section is the **KTU Board-Examiner Favorite**: *"Explain the procedure for registration of [X] in India."* Below are exhaustive, step-by-step procedural walkthroughs for all six IPRs.

---

## 3.1 Procedure for Copyright Registration in India

### Step 1: Determine Eligibility

Confirm that the work falls under **Section 13** of the Copyright Act, 1957:
- Literary works (including computer programs, tables, compilations — Section 2(o))
- Dramatic works
- Musical works (with or without words)
- Artistic works (painting, sculpture, drawing, photograph, architectural work)
- Cinematograph films
- Sound recordings

> **Reason:** Copyright protects *expression*, not *ideas*. The algorithm behind your code is an idea; the source code is the expression.

### Step 2: Prepare Documents

- Two copies of the work
- **Form XIV** (filled in duplicate)
- DD/IPO for the prescribed fee
- **Power of Attorney (Form XII)** if filed through an agent
- NOC from co-authors (if any)

### Step 3: Filing the Application

Submit to the **Register of Copyrights, Copyright Office, Baudhik Sampada Bhawan, New Delhi-110029**, or to the appropriate Branch Office (Calcutta, Chennai, Mumbai, Kolkata).

### Step 4: Diary Number & Waiting Period

The application is allotted a **diary number**. The application is open for **30 days** during which the Registrar may call for objections.

### Step 5: Examination by the Registrar

The Registrar examines the application. If the application is in order, it proceeds to the **Register of Copyrights**.

### Step 6: Issue of Registration Certificate

The Registrar of Copyrights issues the **Extracts from the Register of Copyrights** (the registration certificate) under **Section 49**.

> **Timeline:** ~12–18 months.

---

## 3.2 Procedure for Trademark Registration in India

### Step 1: Trademark Search

Conduct a search in the **Indian Trademark Registry database** (https://ipindiaonline.gov.in) to ensure the mark is not identical or similar to an existing mark.

### Step 2: Filing of Application (Form TM-A)

Submit to the **Trade Marks Registry** at Mumbai, Delhi, Kolkata, Chennai, or Ahmedabad, depending on the applicant's jurisdiction. The application must specify:
- The mark (word, logo, or both)
- The **class(es)** of goods/services (Nice Classification)
- Date of first use (if any)
- Applicant details

### Step 3: Vienna Codification

If the mark contains a logo/device, the Registry classifies it under the **Vienna Agreement** classification system.

### Step 4: Examination by the Examiner

The Trademark Examiner issues an **Examination Report** within **3 months** (statutory). Two outcomes:
- **Accepted (Absolute Grounds)** → application proceeds to publication
- **Objected (Relative Grounds under Section 11)** → applicant must respond within **30 days** (extendable by another 30 days)

### Step 5: Publication in the Trademark Journal

The mark is published in the **Trademark Journal** for **4 months** (statutory opposition period under Section 21).

### Step 6: Opposition Proceedings (if any)

Any "person aggrieved" can file **Notice of Opposition (Form TM-O)** within the 4-month window. Both parties submit evidence; the Registrar decides after a hearing.

### Step 7: Registration

If unopposed or opposition is decided in favor of the applicant, the mark is registered, and a **Registration Certificate (Form TM-2)** is issued. The ® symbol can now be used.

> **Timeline:** ~8–12 months (unopposed); 2–4 years (if opposed).
> **Term:** 10 years, renewable.

---

## 3.3 Procedure for Patent Registration in India

### Step 1: Establishing Inventorship

Identify the **true inventor(s)** under Section 2(1)(y). The right to patent belongs to the **first inventor** (Section 6).

### Step 2: Determine Patentability (Sections 2(1)(j), 2(1)(ja), 2(1)(ac))

Check the **Three Pillars of Patentability**:
- **Novelty** (not part of prior art, anywhere in the world, before the priority date)
- **Inventive Step** (would not be obvious to a person skilled in the art — PSITA)
- **Industrial Application** (capable of being made/used in industry)

> **Note:** Knowledge of the above is essential for answering *"Discuss the criteria for patentability"* in KTU ESE.

### Step 3: Drafting the Patent Specification

Two types:
- **Provisional Specification** (Form 2, with Form 1) — establishes priority date; full description within 12 months
- **Complete Specification** (Form 2) — contains: Title, Field of Invention, Background, Summary, Detailed Description, Claims, Abstract, Drawings

### Step 4: Filing the Application (Form 1 + Form 2 + Form 5)

Pay the prescribed fee. Small entities, startups, and women inventors pay **80% of the standard fee**. Educational institutions pay **standard fee**.

### Step 5: Publication (Section 11A)

The application is published **18 months** after the priority date or filing date. The applicant can request **early publication**.

### Step 6: Request for Examination (Form 18)

Must be filed within **48 months** from the priority date. If not filed, the application is treated as withdrawn.

### Step 7: First Examination Report (FER)

The Controller issues the FER. The applicant has **6 months** (extendable to 12 months) to respond to objections via **Form 3 (Statement of Working)** + amended claims.

### Step 8: Hearing and Grant

After overcoming objections, a hearing is held under **Section 14**, and if satisfied, the patent is **granted** and published in the **Patent Office Journal**.

### Step 9: Renewal

Annual renewal fees from year 3 onwards.

> **Timeline:** ~3–5 years.
> **Term:** 20 years from the date of filing.

### PCT (International) Route

If foreign protection is desired:
- File international application under **PCT** within **12 months** of priority date
- Receive **International Search Report (ISR)** and **Written Opinion**
- Enter **National Phase** in desired countries within **30 months** (for applications filed before 1 April 2002) or **31 months** (for applications filed on/after 1 April 2002)

> **Practical Aspiration for KTU Students:** The PCT route saves 18+ months of separate national filings and provides a preliminary opinion on patentability.

---

## 3.4 Procedure for Geographical Indication (GI) Registration in India

### Step 1: Identify the Producers' Association

Form a **registered association of producers** (or a body established by law). A single individual *cannot* apply. Examples: *Tea Board of India* for Darjeeling Tea; *Banaras Brocades and Sarees Hastkala Vikash Kendra*.

### Step 2: Prepare the Application

- **Form GI-1**: Application form
- **Form GI-2**: Statement of case (defining the GI, goods, geographical area, description of production method, etc.)
- **Form GI-3**: Statement of case (additional class)
- Supporting documents: map, historical evidence, production method, quality characteristics

### Step 3: Filing

Submit to the **Geographical Indications Registry, Intellectual Property Office Building, GST Road, Guindy, Chennai-600032**.

### Step 4: Preliminary Scrutiny

The Registrar scrutinizes the application within **3 months** for any defects.

### Step 5: Examination and Publication

If accepted, the application is published in the **GI Journal** with a **3-month opposition window**.

### Step 6: Opposition (if any)

Any person can file an opposition within 3 months.

### Step 7: Registration

If unopposed, the GI is registered, and a **Registration Certificate** is issued.

> **Timeline:** ~12–18 months.
> **Term:** 10 years, renewable.

---

## 3.5 Procedure for Trade Secret Protection (No Registration)

### Step 1: Identify the Trade Secret

Determine what constitutes the trade secret — formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process.

### Step 2: Implement Reasonable Security Measures

- **Physical Security:** Locked rooms, restricted access, CCTV
- **Digital Security:** Encryption, password policies, firewalls, DLP (Data Loss Prevention)
- **Legal Security:** **Non-Disclosure Agreements (NDAs)** with employees, contractors, vendors
- **HR Security:** Non-compete clauses, exit interviews, return of confidential information

### Step 3: Mark Documents as "Confidential"

Use visible markings, headers, footers, and contractual clauses.

### Step 4: Monitor and Enforce

- Maintain audit trails
- Take immediate legal action (injunction + damages) if breach is detected under the **Indian Contract Act, 1872** (Sections 27, 73) and **Information Technology Act, 2000**

> **Note:** The trade secret right ends the moment the information becomes public knowledge through no fault of the owner (e.g., independent reverse engineering, which is a legitimate means under common law).

---

## 3.6 Procedure for Industrial Design Registration in India

### Step 1: Confirm Eligibility

The design must be:
- Applied to a **functional article** (not a painting or pure artwork)
- **New** or **original**
- **Visible** during normal use
- Not solely dictated by function
- Not contrary to public order/morality

### Step 2: Prepare Application

- **Form 1**
- Four copies of the design (drawings/photographs from six angles)
- Statement of novelty
- Power of Attorney (Form 15/16)
- Priority document (if claiming Paris Convention priority)

### Step 3: Filing with the Patent Office (Designs Wing)

File at **Kolkata, Mumbai, Delhi, or Chennai**.

### Step 4: Examination

The Controller examines for novelty, originality, and compliance with the Act. If accepted, registration is granted.

### Step 5: Publication in the Design Register

### Step 6: Issuance of Certificate of Registration

> **Timeline:** ~6–12 months.
> **Term:** 10 years, extendable to 15 years.

### International Route: Hague Agreement (Geneva Act, 1999)

India is a member. Applicants can file a single **international design application** designating multiple countries, with **initial 5-year protection** renewable in tranches up to 15 years.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Discuss the procedural steps for the registration of a Patent in India. Explain the criteria of patentability with reference to relevant sections of the Patents Act, 1970.** [14 Marks]

#### (a) Criteria of Patentability [7 Marks]

The Patents Act, 1970, under **Section 2(1)(j)** defines an "invention" as a new product or process involving an inventive step and capable of industrial application. The three substantive criteria are:

1. **Novelty (Section 2(1)(l))** — The invention must not form part of the **prior art** anywhere in the world before the **priority date**. Prior art includes any matter made available to the public by written/oral disclosure, use, or publication.

2. **Inventive Step / Non-Obviousness (Section 2(1)(ja))** — The invention must not be obvious to a **Person Skilled in the Art (PSITA)**. The Supreme Court in *Novartis v. Union of India (2013)* tightened the standard, requiring "enhanced efficacy" for pharmaceutical patents.

3. **Industrial Application / Utility (Section 2(1)(ac))** — The invention must be capable of being made or used in industry (including agriculture).

**Non-Patentable Subject Matter (Sections 3 and 4):** Frivolous inventions, inventions contrary to public order or morality, mere discoveries of scientific principles, atomic energy-related inventions, plants/animals (other than microorganisms), mathematical or business methods, mental or religious methods, traditional knowledge, and inventions prejudicial to public health.

#### (b) Procedural Steps for Patent Registration [7 Marks]

1. **Step 1 — Identification of Inventors** under Section 2(1)(y). The first inventor (or assignee) has the right to the patent.

2. **Step 2 — Filing the Application** using **Form 1** (Application), **Form 2** (Provisional or Complete Specification), and **Form 5** (Declaration of Inventorship). Fees: ₹1,600 for natural persons (e-filing), reduced to ₹1,600 from ₹8,000 for legal entities; 80% rebate for startups, small entities, and women.

3. **Step 3 — Publication** under Section 11A, automatically after 18 months, or earlier on request.

4. **Step 4 — Request for Examination** using **Form 18** within **48 months** of the priority date.

5. **Step 5 — First Examination Report (FER)** issued by the Controller, requiring response within 6 to 12 months.

6. **Step 6 — Hearing under Section 14** if objections persist.

7. **Step 7 — Grant and Publication** in the Patent Office Journal.

8. **Step 8 — Annual Renewal** from the third year onward.

9. **Step 9 — PCT Route (if foreign protection desired):** File PCT application within 12 months, receive ISR, enter national phase within 30/31 months.

> **Term:** 20 years from the date of filing.

---

### Question Choice B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Explain the procedure for registration of Trademarks in India. Discuss the role of international treaties like the Madrid Protocol in obtaining trademark protection abroad.** [14 Marks]

#### (a) Procedure for Trademark Registration in India [7 Marks]

1. **Step 1 — Trademark Search** in the Indian IP database to ensure distinctiveness and non-conflict with existing marks.

2. **Step 2 — Filing Form TM-A** at the Trade Marks Registry (Mumbai, Delhi, Kolkata, Chennai, or Ahmedabad) along with the prescribed fee (₹4,500 e-filing for individual/startup). Specify the **Nice Classification class** (1–34 for goods, 35–45 for services).

3. **Step 3 — Vienna Codification** if the mark contains a logo.

4. **Step 4 — Examination** by the Examiner within **3 months** of filing. Possible outcomes:
   - **Accepted** → proceeds to publication
   - **Objected (Section 9 absolute grounds or Section 11 relative grounds)** → applicant must file a response within 30 days (extendable to 60 days).

5. **Step 5 — Publication in the Trademark Journal** for a **4-month opposition window** under Section 21.

6. **Step 6 — Opposition Proceedings** — Any aggrieved person may file **Form TM-O**. Hearing held; Registrar decides.

7. **Step 7 — Registration** — If unopposed, **Form TM-2 (Registration Certificate)** is issued. Applicant can now use the **® symbol**.

8. **Step 8 — Renewal** every 10 years indefinitely.

#### (b) Madrid Protocol for International Protection [7 Marks]

The **Madrid Protocol (1989)** is an international treaty administered by **WIPO (World Intellectual Property Organization)** that simplifies the process of seeking trademark protection in multiple countries. India acceded to the Madrid Protocol on **8 July 2013**.

**Key Features:**
- **Single Application:** A trademark owner can file a single international application (in one language, with one set of fees) at the **International Bureau (IB) of WIPO** in Geneva.
- **Designation of Member Countries:** The applicant can designate any of the **110+ Madrid Union member countries** where protection is sought.
- **Office of Origin:** The application is first filed in the **national/regional office** of the applicant's home country (e.g., Indian Trade Marks Registry) for a **basic mark**, and then extended to the international level.
- **Examination by National Offices:** Each designated country's national office conducts its own substantive examination, applying its own laws.
- **Centralized Management:** Subsequent changes (assignment, renewal, address change) can be recorded in one place with effect in all designated countries.
- **Term:** International registration is valid for **10 years**, renewable for another 10 years indefinitely.
- **Fees:** Comprises a basic fee (in Swiss Francs), a designation fee per country, and a complementary fee based on class numbers.

> **Practical Benefit:** Eliminates the need for separate national filings, reduces cost, simplifies management, and provides a single procedural framework. For KTU students aspiring to file trademarks abroad, Madrid is the most economical route.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Copyright and Patent (most common error):** Copyright = expression (automatic, 60 years post-life). Patent = invention (20 years, requires registration). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this mix-up.
> 2. **Forgetting the international treaty name:** Always mention **PCT for patents, Madrid for trademarks, Hague for designs, Berne for copyright, Lisbon for GIs**.
> 3. **Wrong timelines:** 20 years (patent), 10 years renewable (trademark/GI), 15 years max (design). Examiners cross-check.
> 4. **Misspellings of statutory terms:** "Trademarks" (not "Trade Marks" or "Trademarks Act 1999" without the *s*). Correct: **Trade Marks Act, 1999**.
> 5. **Treating Trade Secrets as registrable:** Trade secrets have **NO formal registration**. The protection is purely contractual and equitable. Examiners penalize writing "Form TS-1" or any such form.
> 6. **GI owner error:** Saying "any individual farmer can apply" is wrong. Only a **producers' association** can apply for a GI.
> 7. **Not mentioning Section numbers in patent questions:** Examiners expect Section 2(1)(j), 2(1)(ja), 2(1)(ac) for criteria and Section 11A/14 for procedure.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Copyright (1957)** — Automatic; Form XIV; Life + 60 years; Berne Convention; protects expression, not ideas.
- **Trade Mark (1999)** — Form TM-A; 10 years renewable indefinitely; Nice 45 classes; Madrid Protocol for international; ® symbol.
- **Patent (1970)** — Form 1 + 2 + 5; **20 years**; criteria = Novelty + Inventive Step + Industrial Application; PCT route.
- **GI (1999)** — Form GI-1; producers' association only; Darjeeling Tea, Kanchipuram Silk, Alphonso Mango, Palakkadan Matta Rice; 10 years renewable; Lisbon Agreement.
- **Trade Secret** — **No statute, no registration**; protected by NDAs, equity, common law, IT Act 2000; Coca-Cola formula, KFC recipe.
- **Industrial Design (2000)** — Form 1; 10 years + 5 years extension (15 years max); Locarno 32 classes; Hague Agreement.
- **WIPO** administers Madrid, PCT, Hague, Lisbon, Budapest Treaties.
- **TRIPS (1995)** is the **baseline international IP standard** enforced by WTO membership.
- **First-to-file** rule applies to Patents, Trademarks, GIs, and Designs in India (unlike US patent law which historically was first-to-invent).
- **Digital India:** Online filing available at https://ipindiaonline.gov.in for all six IPRs.
- **Startup India / DPIIT recognition** entitles to **80% patent fee rebate** and **fast-track examination**.
- **Priority Date** is the *date of first filing* — critical for international protection under the Paris Convention (6 months for trademarks/designs, 12 months for patents/GIs).
- **Madrid Protocol** (Trademarks), **PCT** (Patents), **Hague** (Designs), **Lisbon** (GIs) — remember these four international filing systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
