Digital Products and Law: Digital Innovations and Developments as Knowledge Assets, IP Laws, Cyber Law and Digital Content Protection, Unfair Competition, Meaning and Relationship between Unfair Competition and IP Laws, Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT744 - PATENTS AND IPR
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Digital Products and Law: Digital Innovations and Developments as Knowledge Assets, IP Laws, Cyber Law and Digital Content Protection, Unfair Competition, Meaning and Relationship between Unfair Competition and IP Laws, Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Digital Products as Knowledge Assets

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
A *Digital Product* is any intangible good that exists in binary form, is stored, distributed, and consumed via electronic or digital networks. When a digital product embodies original intellectual creation, technical innovation, or commercially valuable information, it qualifies as a *Knowledge Asset* — a legally recognized intangible property that can be owned, licensed, traded, and protected under Intellectual Property (IP) regimes.

The **World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO)** defines Knowledge Assets in the digital context as *creations of the mind in the digital environment, including software, databases, multimedia content, AI models, algorithms, and digital designs, that have economic value and are capable of legal protection.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For examination purposes, students must remember that digital products are **NOT** automatically protected — protection requires mapping the innovation to the correct IP regime (Copyright, Patent, Trademark, Trade Secret, or Design).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a digital product like a recipe for a Michelin-star dish. The recipe (the code, the algorithm, the dataset) is an instruction set — you can copy it perfectly, you can give it to millions of people at zero marginal cost, and the original chef loses nothing physically. Yet the chef invested years of training, experimentation, and creativity. IP law is the legal "strainer" that catches unauthorized copies and rewards the creator — without it, digital products would be impossible to monetize, and innovation would collapse under free-riding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A *digital product* is the deliverable (the .exe file, the .mp4, the .pdf). A *knowledge asset* is the legal-economic recognition of the embedded intellectual value. The same product can be both a digital product AND a knowledge asset simultaneously.

---

### 1.2 Cyber Law and the Indian Legal Framework

**Formal Definition:**
*Cyber Law* is the body of legal regulations governing digital communications, electronic transactions, data privacy, cybercrime, and the protection of digital content over computer networks and the Internet.

The principal statute in India is the **Information Technology Act, 2000 (IT Act, 2000)**, amended by the **Information Technology (Amendment) Act, 2008**, supplemented by the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act)**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
If the Internet is a city, then Cyber Law is its police force + constitution. Just as physical property laws define what you can do with a house, Cyber Law defines what you can do with data, websites, digital signatures, and online content. The IT Act 2000 is essentially the *Indian Penal Code for cyberspace*.

---

### 1.3 Digital Content Protection

**Formal Definition:**
*Digital Content Protection* refers to the legal and technological mechanisms (copyright, DRM, watermarking, licensing) that prevent unauthorized reproduction, distribution, modification, or commercial exploitation of digital works such as software, music, video, e-books, databases, and AI-generated content.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine publishing a book where, every time someone photocopied it, the photocopier self-destructed. That is the *intent* of Digital Rights Management (DRM). Digital Content Protection is the combination of legal rules (you cannot copy) and technical tools (you cannot easily copy) that together protect creator revenue.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Anchor:** Digital content protection sits at the intersection of **Copyright Law** (legal layer) and **Technological Protection Measures / TPMs** (technical layer). KTU examiners frequently test this layered structure.

---

### 1.4 Unfair Competition

**Formal Definition (WIPO/Paris Convention Standard):**
*Unfair Competition* is any act of competition that is contrary to honest practices in industrial or commercial matters. Article 10bis of the **Paris Convention for the Protection of Industrial Property (1883)** defines it as acts that create confusion, discredit competitors, or mislead the public regarding the source, nature, or quality of goods or services.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Unfair Competition is the legal equivalent of "foul play" in a sports match — even if no written rule is broken, the spirit of fair play is violated. Examples include passing off a look-alike product, bribing critics to give false reviews, or stealing trade secrets through industrial espionage. It is broader than any single IP right and acts as a *catch-all* for dishonest commercial behavior.

---

### 1.5 Relationship Between Unfair Competition and IP Laws

**Formal Definition:**
Unfair Competition and IP Laws share a common objective — *protecting honest commercial practices and rewarding creativity/innovation* — but differ in scope. IP Laws grant *exclusive rights* (positive entitlements), whereas Unfair Competition Law provides *defensive protection* against dishonest acts that harm competitors or consumers, even when no specific IP right is infringed.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of IP Laws as a fortress with multiple specialized walls (Copyright Wall, Patent Wall, Trademark Wall, Trade Secret Wall). Unfair Competition is the *patrol guard* that walks the perimeter — it catches intruders that slip through the gaps between the walls (acts that are dishonest but don't violate any specific IP statute).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Analytical Matrix

### 2.1 Classification of Digital Innovations as Knowledge Assets

Digital innovations map to IP regimes based on their **nature, function, and statutory eligibility**. The decision tree below is the standard KTU 2024 analytical framework:

**Step 1: Identify the Nature of the Digital Innovation**
- **Functional / Technical** (solves a problem) → Patent eligibility check
- **Expressive / Aesthetic** (original expression) → Copyright
- **Source Identifier** (brand, logo, domain name) → Trademark
- **Confidential / Commercial Value** (not public) → Trade Secret
- **Visual Appearance** (UI design, icon, GUI) → Industrial Design

**Step 2: Check Statutory Eligibility**
- **Patents** require novelty, inventive step, industrial application, and (in India) must NOT be a computer program *per se* — though embedded technical applications may qualify.
- **Copyright** arises automatically on creation; no registration required in India (registration is optional but evidentiary).
- **Trademarks** require distinctiveness and graphical representation; registration under the Trade Marks Act, 1999.
- **Trade Secrets** require reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy; protected under common law and the Indian Contract Act, 1872.
- **Designs** must be new and applied to an article by industrial process; registered under the Designs Act, 2000.

**Step 3: Determine the Layered Protection Strategy**
Most digital products receive **multiple overlapping protections** simultaneously — this is the *Layered IP Strategy*.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Analytical Matrix: IP Regimes vs. Digital Products

| Digital Product Type | Primary IP Regime | Secondary IP Regime | Statutory Basis (India) | Duration / Term |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Software (source/object code) | Copyright | Trade Secret (for algorithms) | Copyright Act, 1957 | **Life + 60 years** |
| Software with technical invention | Patent (if novel) | Copyright | Patents Act, 1970 | **20 years** |
| UI / GUI Design | Design + Copyright | — | Designs Act, 2000; Copyright Act, 1957 | Design: **10–15 yrs**; Copyright: **Life + 60 yrs** |
| Database (compilation) | Copyright (sui generis in EU; India: Copyright Act) | Contract law | Copyright Act, 1957 | **Life + 60 years** |
| AI/ML Model Weights | Trade Secret | Copyright (if code) | Common law + Contract | Indefinite (if secrecy maintained) |
| Brand Name / Logo / Domain | Trademark | — | Trade Marks Act, 1999 | **10 years (renewable)** |
| Music / Video / e-Book | Copyright | DRM/TPMs | Copyright Act, 1957 | **Life + 60 years** |
| Source Code (unpublished) | Trade Secret | Copyright | Indian Contract Act, 1872 | Indefinite |
| Web Page Layout | Copyright | Design | Copyright Act, 1957 | **Life + 60 years** |
| 3D Printing Design File | Copyright + Design | Patent (if functional) | Copyright Act; Designs Act; Patents Act | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Point:** Software in India receives **dual protection** — the *literal code* is protected as a literary work under Copyright, while a *novel underlying technical process* may be patented. Examiners test whether students confuse these two layers.

---

### 2.3 Analytical Framework: Unfair Competition vs. IP Rights

| Parameter | IP Law | Unfair Competition Law |
|---|---|---|
| **Source of Rights** | Statutory grant (Copyright Act, Patent Act, etc.) | Paris Convention Art. 10bis + Common law torts (passing off) |
| **Nature of Right** | Positive / Exclusive right to exploit | Defensive / Negative right against dishonest acts |
| **Requirement of Registration** | Mostly yes (Patents, Trademarks, Designs); Copyright auto | Generally no |
| **Subject Matter** | Specific categories (works, inventions, marks, designs) | Any commercial act contrary to honest practice |
| **Scope** | Narrow and defined | Broad and flexible (catch-all) |
| **Remedy for Misuse** | Infringement suit (statutory) | Passing off / unfair competition suit (common law) |
| **Territoriality** | Strictly territorial | Often extraterritorial under Paris Convention |
| **Overlap with Other IP** | Each IP is distinct | Subsumes gaps between IP rights |
| **Indian Legal Basis** | Specific statutes | Common law tort of passing off; CPC 1908 for injunctions |
| **Example** | Pirating software = Copyright infringement | Selling look-alike packaging = Passing off |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Tech Industries

The framework above is operationalized in the following industrial contexts:

1. **Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) Companies** — Use layered IP (Copyright + Trade Secret + Trademark) to protect their codebase, algorithms, and brand.
2. **AI/ML Startups** — Rely heavily on Trade Secret protection since patents disclose and algorithms may lack inventive step.
3. **E-Commerce Platforms** — Use Trademark law for brands, Unfair Competition law to fight counterfeit listings, and Cyber Law against phishing.
4. **Media & Entertainment** — Combine Copyright + DRM + Unfair Competition to combat piracy.
5. **Fintech** — Patent core cryptographic methods, copyright UI, trademark brand, and use Trade Secret for proprietary fraud-detection algorithms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For B.Tech students, the practical takeaway is that *every line of code you write, every design you create, and every algorithm you invent* is automatically a knowledge asset — your awareness of which regime protects it determines whether your innovation is monetizable or freely copyable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Case Study Frameworks

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Mapping a Digital Product to IP Protection

**Problem Statement:** A Kerala-based B.Tech student develops a novel machine-learning algorithm that detects diabetic retinopathy from retinal images, deploys it as a mobile app called "RetinaGuard" with a unique logo, and uploads the training dataset of 50,000 anonymized images to a server. Identify the IP regimes applicable.

**Step 1: Decompose the Digital Product into Constituents**

The product consists of four distinct constituents:
- (i) The ML algorithm (functional/mathematical)
- (ii) The source code (textual/literal expression)
- (iii) The mobile app "RetinaGuard" with logo (brand identity)
- (iv) The training dataset (compilation of data)

**Step 2: Apply the IP Mapping Function for Each Constituent**

For the ML algorithm:
- The algorithm is a *method* of processing data. Under Indian law, algorithms and business methods are excluded from patentability under **Section 3(k) of the Patents Act, 1970**. However, if the algorithm produces a *technical effect* (here: medical diagnosis from images), it may be patentable as a *technical application* (per the *Ferid Allani* line of cases at the Delhi High Court).
- Trade Secret protection is available if the algorithm's internal weights and architecture are kept confidential.
- The *expression* of the algorithm in code is protected by Copyright.

For the source code:
- Under **Section 13(1)(a) of the Copyright Act, 1957**, source code qualifies as a "literary work".
- Copyright arises automatically upon creation in a tangible medium.
- The student is the first owner under **Section 17** of the Copyright Act, 1957.

For the app name "RetinaGuard" and its logo:
- The name is registrable as a trademark under **Section 9 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999** if it is distinctive and not descriptive of the goods.
- The logo is registrable as a device mark.
- Both are eligible for registration in Class 9 (software) and Class 42 (SaaS/medical services).

For the training dataset:
- A database is protected under **Section 2(o) of the Copyright Act, 1957** as a "compilation" if there is originality in the selection or arrangement.
- Under the **DPDP Act, 2023**, processing of personal data (if any) requires consent and purpose limitation; anonymized data is exempt.
- If the dataset represents a *substantial investment*, future sui generis protection may emerge (India currently has no Database Right as in the EU).

**Step 3: Synthesize the Layered Protection Strategy**

$$
\text{Total Protection} = \text{Copyright (code)} + \text{Patent (if technical effect)} + \text{Trade Secret (algorithm weights)} + \text{Trademark (brand)} + \text{Design (UI)}
$$

**Step 4: Identify Vulnerabilities**
- The dataset, if reverse-engineered, exposes the algorithm's logic.
- Publishing the code under an open-source license (MIT, GPL) would extinguish trade secret protection.
- The brand "RetinaGuard" must be distinctive; a purely descriptive name would be refused registration.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation: The Relationship Between IP and Unfair Competition

**Step 1: Establish the Conceptual Overlap**

Both IP Law and Unfair Competition Law share the foundational principle of *protecting honest commercial practices and rewarding innovation*. This principle is explicitly stated in **Article 10bis of the Paris Convention**, to which India became a member in 1998 (via the TRIPS Agreement, 1995).

**Step 2: Distinguish the Operative Mechanisms**

IP Law grants a *positive right*:
$$
\text{IP Right} = f(\text{Statutory Grant}, \text{Exclusivity}, \text{Defined Subject Matter})
$$

Unfair Competition provides a *defensive remedy*:
$$
\text{Unfair Competition Claim} = f(\text{Honest Practice Violation}, \text{Likelihood of Confusion}, \text{Misleading Conduct})
$$

**Step 3: Demonstrate the Complementary Function**

When an act violates a specific IP right (e.g., copying software), the plaintiff sues for *IP infringement*. When an act is dishonest but does not fall within any IP statute (e.g., misappropriating a competitor's customer list through a former employee's breach of confidence), the plaintiff sues for *unfair competition / breach of confidence*.

$$
\text{IP Coverage} \cup \text{Unfair Competition Coverage} = \text{Total Protection of Commercial Integrity}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the Three-Step WIPO Test for Unfair Competition**

The WIPO Model Provisions on Unfair Competition (1996) provide a three-pronged test:

**Test 1 — Confusion:** Any act that creates or is likely to create confusion regarding the source, sponsorship, affiliation, or identity of goods or services. *Example:* Selling "Cocacolla" with a red-and-white label.

**Test 2 — Discredit:** False allegations that discredit a competitor's goods or services. *Example:* Spreading rumors that a competitor's app contains malware.

**Test 3 — Misleading:** Misleading the public regarding the nature, characteristics, or qualities of goods. *Example:* Advertising an e-book as "AI-generated" when it is human-written.

If any one of the three is satisfied, the act constitutes Unfair Competition under WIPO standards.

---

### 3.3 Case Study Frameworks (KTU 2024 Standard)

**Case Study 1: Microsoft Corporation v. Yogesh Papat (2005, Delhi High Court)**

*Facts:* The defendant sold pirated Microsoft software bundled with cheap computers.
*Issue:* Whether sale of pirated software constitutes copyright infringement and whether the plaintiff's rights extend to OEM licensing.
*Holding:* The Court held that unauthorized reproduction and distribution of software constitutes copyright infringement under the Copyright Act, 1957, and that software license terms bind the end-user.
*IP Regime Tested:* Copyright + Contractual licensing (EULA).
*Unfair Competition Element:* Passing off — buyers were led to believe they were buying genuine Microsoft products.

**Case Study 2: Yahoo! Inc. v. Akash Arora (1999, Delhi High Court)**

*Facts:* The defendant registered the domain name "yahooindia.com" and used a similar interface to Yahoo!.
*Issue:* Whether use of a similar domain name and trade dress constitutes passing off under Indian law, in the absence of trademark registration in India at the time.
*Holding:* The Court applied the *common law tort of passing off* — the plaintiff need not have a registered trademark; goodwill and reputation in India were sufficient. The defendant was restrained from using the domain.
*IP Regime Tested:* Trademark (common law) + Unfair Competition.
*Ratio:* Passing off protects unregistered goodwill — this is the bridge between IP and Unfair Competition law.

**Case Study 3: Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak (2008, Supreme Court of India)**

*Facts:* The defendant copied the headnotes, editorial notes, and case arrangement of the plaintiff's legal database (SCC Online).
*Issue:* Whether a database (compilation) is protected by copyright when the underlying judicial decisions are public domain.
*Holding:* The Supreme Court held that *originality in selection, arrangement, and presentation* is sufficient for copyright protection. The plaintiff's editorial contributions were protected.
*IP Regime Tested:* Copyright in compilations (Section 2(o) of the Copyright Act, 1957).
*Unfair Competition Element:* The defendant's copying was a "slavish imitation" — a species of unfair competition.

**Case Study 4: Christian Louboutin v. Nakul Bajaj (2018, Delhi High Court)**

*Facts:* The defendant sold red-soled shoes that mimicked Louboutin's signature design.
*Issue:* Whether a single color used in a specific position can function as a trademark.
*Holding:* The Court held that a distinctive combination (red lacquer on the sole) acquired secondary meaning and qualified as a trademark. Injunction granted.
*IP Regime Tested:* Trademark + Design + Unfair Competition.
*Ratio:* Even without copying the brand name, mimicking distinctive trade dress constitutes unfair competition.

**Case Study 5: In re: Bilski (US, 2010) — Comparative Reference**

*Facts:* Bilski's patent claim was for a method of hedging risks in commodities trading — a pure business method.
*Issue:* Whether abstract business methods are patentable.
*Holding:* The US Supreme Court held that abstract ideas are not patentable, but a concrete application with a technological improvement may be.
*Comparative Insight for KTU:* Indian Section 3(k) of the Patents Act, 1970 mirrors this principle — software *per se* is excluded, but technical applications are not.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture Flow: Digital Product → Knowledge Asset → IP Protection

```mermaid

    A[Digital Innovation] --> B{Nature of Creation?}
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A: 3-Mark Questions**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define "Digital Knowledge Asset" with two examples.*

**Model Answer:**
A Digital Knowledge Asset is a legally recognized intangible creation of the mind in digital form, possessing economic value and capable of protection under IP regimes. It includes software code, databases, AI models, digital content, and UI designs.
*Example 1:* The source code of a mobile application, protected automatically by Copyright.
*Example 2:* A trained AI model whose internal weights are protected as a Trade Secret.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between Copyright and Patent protection for software.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Copyright | Patent |
|---|---|---|
| **Subject Matter** | The literal expression of the code | The underlying technical process/method |
| **Automatically Available?** | Yes, on creation | No, requires application and grant |
| **Term** | Life of author + 60 years | 20 years from filing |
| **Indian Provision** | Section 13(1)(a), Copyright Act, 1957 | Section 3(k) excludes software *per se*; technical applications may qualify, Patents Act, 1970 |
| **Idea vs Expression** | Protects expression, not idea | Protects functional invention |

---

### **Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of Unfair Competition as defined under the Paris Convention. Describe the three tests established by the WIPO Model Provisions to determine whether an act constitutes Unfair Competition.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the relationship between Unfair Competition Law and Intellectual Property Law. Use the *Yahoo! v. Akash Arora* case to illustrate how Unfair Competition complements Trademark protection.

**Model Answer (a):**

*Concept of Unfair Competition:*
Article 10bis of the **Paris Convention for the Protection of Industrial Property (1883)** defines Unfair Competition as any act of competition contrary to honest practices in industrial or commercial matters. The WIPO Model Provisions on Unfair Competition (1996) further operationalize this definition.

*The Three WIPO Tests:*

**Test 1 — Causing Confusion:**
Any act that creates or is likely to create confusion regarding the source, sponsorship, affiliation, or identity of goods or services. The test is applied from the perspective of an *ordinary consumer* (the "man of average intelligence and imperfect recollection" standard). Example: Selling a beverage with a red-and-white label and a name like "Coca Colla" constitutes confusion.

**Test 2 — False Allegations Discrediting Competitors:**
Any false allegation that discredits a competitor's goods, services, or commercial activities. The plaintiff must show the statement is false, made with malice or reckless disregard, and causes commercial harm. Example: Publishing fake reviews claiming a competitor's app contains malware.

**Test 3 — Misleading Public:**
Any act that misleads the public regarding the nature, manufacturing process, characteristics, suitability, or quantity of goods. Example: Advertising a product as "Made in Germany" when it is made in China.

*Remedies:* Injunctions, damages, accounts of profits, and destruction of infringing goods.

**Model Answer (b):**

*Relationship Between Unfair Competition and IP Law:*

Both IP Law and Unfair Competition Law share the foundational objective of *protecting honest commercial practices, rewarding innovation, and preventing consumer deception*. However, they differ in mechanism:

- **IP Law** grants *positive exclusive rights* (e.g., the right to reproduce, distribute, license).
- **Unfair Competition Law** provides *defensive remedies* against acts that are dishonest but may not violate any specific IP statute.

Unfair Competition functions as a *gap-filler* — it covers commercial misconduct that falls between the specialized IP regimes. For example, misappropriation of a trade secret (not registered as a patent) is actionable under Unfair Competition even though no specific IP statute (Copyright or Patent) is violated.

*Case Illustration — Yahoo! Inc. v. Akash Arora (1999, Delhi HC):*
Yahoo! did not have a registered trademark in India when the defendant Akash Arora registered "yahooindia.com" and created a look-alike portal. The defendant argued that without registration, no IP right was infringed. The Court rejected this and held that the *common law tort of passing off* protects unregistered goodwill and reputation. The Court granted an injunction, recognizing that IP registration is not a precondition for protection against unfair competition.

This case demonstrates that **Unfair Competition Law extends protection beyond statutory IP rights**, ensuring that commercial integrity is preserved even when formal IP registration is absent.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** "Digital products require layered IP protection." Discuss this statement with reference to software, databases, and mobile applications. Cite relevant statutory provisions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Outline the key provisions of the **Information Technology Act, 2000** relevant to digital content protection. Briefly mention the *Shreya Singhal v. Union of India* (2015) judgment.

**Model Answer (a):**

*Layered IP Protection for Digital Products:*

Digital products typically involve multiple creative and functional elements, each eligible for a different IP regime. Reliance on a single IP right leaves critical assets unprotected.

**1. Software:**
- *Copyright* protects the literal source code as a "literary work" under **Section 13(1)(a) of the Copyright Act, 1957**. Term: Life + 60 years.
- *Trade Secret* protects proprietary algorithms, internal logic, and unreleased code under the **Indian Contract Act, 1872** (NDAs and confidentiality clauses).
- *Patent* protects a *technical process* embodied in the software under the **Patents Act, 1970**, but not the algorithm *per se* (Section 3(k) exclusion).
- *Trademark* protects the software's brand name and logo under the **Trade Marks Act, 1999**.

**2. Databases:**
- *Copyright* protects the database as a "compilation" under **Section 2(o) of the Copyright Act, 1957** if there is originality in selection or arrangement (per *Eastern Book Co. v. D.B. Modak*, 2008).
- *Contract Law* protects licensed access through End-User License Agreements.
- *Sui generis Database Right* is not yet recognized in India (unlike the EU Database Directive, 96/9/EC).

**3. Mobile Applications:**
- *Copyright* protects source code, text content, images, and audio.
- *Design* protects the UI layout and icons under the **Designs Act, 2000**.
- *Trademark* protects the app name, logo, and brand colors.
- *Trade Secret* protects the business logic and proprietary algorithms.
- *Patent* may protect novel in-app processes (e.g., novel image-processing pipelines).

*Conclusion:* Layered IP protection ensures that the *literal, functional, visual, and brand* elements of a digital product are each independently enforceable.

**Model Answer (b):**

*Key Provisions of the IT Act, 2000 (as amended in 2008):*

**Section 43 — Compensation for damage to computer system:** Civil liability for unauthorized access, downloading, introducing viruses, or disrupting a computer. The aggrieved party can claim damages up to ₹5 crore.

**Section 65 — Tampering with computer source documents:** Criminal liability — imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.

**Section 66 — Computer-related offences (Hacking):** Unauthorized access with intent to cause damage or steal data. Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine up to ₹2 lakh, or both.

**Section 66C — Identity Theft:** Fraudulently or dishonestly using another person's electronic signature, password, or unique identification feature. Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹1 lakh.

**Section 66D — Cheating by personation using computer resource:** Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹1 lakh.

**Section 66E — Violation of privacy:** Publishing or transmitting images of a person's private parts without consent. Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine.

**Section 67 — Publishing obscene material in electronic form:** First offence: up to 3 years + fine. Subsequent: up to 5 years + fine.

**Section 67B — Child pornography:** Imprisonment up to 7 years + fine.

**Section 69 — Interception, monitoring, decryption:** Empowers the government to intercept, monitor, or decrypt content for national security.

**Section 79 — Safe harbour for intermediaries:** Intermediaries (e.g., ISPs, social media platforms) are not liable for third-party content if they observe due diligence.

*Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015) — Supreme Court:*
The Supreme Court struck down **Section 66A** of the IT Act, holding it violative of **Article 19(1)(a)** of the Constitution (freedom of speech). The Court ruled that vague terms like "offensive" and "menacing" gave the State unbridled power to restrict online speech. The judgment reaffirmed the *reasonable restrictions* test under **Article 19(2)** and upheld the constitutionality of Sections 69A (blocking orders) and 79 (intermediary safe harbour), reading them down with safeguards.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Confusing Copyright and Patent in software questions:** Many students write that "software is patented." Wrong. Software *per se* is excluded under Section 3(k) of the Patents Act, 1970. Only technical applications qualify. The code is *copyrighted*, not patented.
> 2. **Forgetting the Paris Convention:** When asked about Unfair Competition, students often skip Article 10bis. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Treating Copyright as requiring registration:** In India, Copyright arises *automatically* under Section 13 of the Copyright Act, 1957. Registration is *not* mandatory (but is evidentiary).
> 4. **Misstating the WIPO Three Tests:** The three tests are (i) Confusion, (ii) Discrediting, (iii) Misleading — not "piracy, counterfeiting, infringement."
> 5. **Ignoring the Layered IP strategy:** When a digital product question appears, mentioning only ONE IP regime loses 3–4 marks. Always list at least two.
> 6. **Confusing the IT Act 2000 with the DPDP Act 2023:** The IT Act 2000 governs cybercrime and e-commerce; the DPDP Act 2023 governs personal data processing. They are *complementary*, not substitutes.
> 7. **Omitting the *Shreya Singhal* case in Cyber Law questions:** A 14-mark question on Cyber Law that omits this landmark judgment is incomplete.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Digital Product** = a binary deliverable; **Knowledge Asset** = the legal-economic recognition of its embedded value. They are NOT synonymous.
- **Indian IP Statutes at a glance:**
  - Copyright Act, 1957 (term: **Life + 60 years**)
  - Patents Act, 1970 (term: **20 years**)
  - Trade Marks Act, 1999 (term: **10 years, renewable**)
  - Designs Act, 2000 (term: **10 years + 5-year extension**)
  - IT Act, 2000 + DPDP Act, 2023 (Cyber Law)
- **Section 3(k), Patents Act, 1970** excludes computer programs *per se* — but technical applications of software MAY be patentable.
- **Copyright is automatic in India** under Section 13 of the Copyright Act, 1957. Registration is optional.
- **Trade Secret protection** requires *reasonable efforts* to maintain secrecy (NDA, password protection, restricted access).
- **Layered IP Strategy** is the industry standard — never rely on a single IP regime for a complex digital product.
- **Unfair Competition = Paris Convention Article 10bis + WIPO Three Tests** (Confusion, Discredit, Misleading).
- **Passing off (Indian common law tort)** protects unregistered goodwill — illustrated in *Yahoo! v. Akash Arora (1999)*.
- **Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak (2008)** established that databases are protected by Copyright if there is originality in selection/arrangement.
- **Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015)** struck down Section 66A of the IT Act as unconstitutional — a landmark for digital free speech in India.
- **DPDP Act, 2023** is India's first comprehensive data protection statute, replacing the *Personal Data Protection Bill, 2019*. Maximum penalty: **₹250 crore per breach**.
- **The relationship between Unfair Competition and IP Law** is *complementary and gap-filling*: IP grants exclusive rights; Unfair Competition defends against residual dishonest acts.
- **WIPO Model Provisions (1996)** are the standard international reference for Unfair Competition — examiners expect this citation.
